摘要顯示有以人工語言預訓練並以 probing 分析評估結構/位置依賴性對自然語言的可遷移性（兩位初審亦認為具實證語法分析方向），但僅提及“neural network encoders”且未明確指出為 Transformer 架構，缺少關鍵的模型架構證據，故評為 3（需更多證據再決定） 。
兩位 junior reviewer 均判為 3；摘要顯示該工作在六個 transformer 模型上以打分與可視化評估上下文化現象並驗證計算語言學假說，但未明確聲稱對句法結構或句法知識做出實證分析，證據不足—因此給予 3（不確定/需要更多證據）。

The abstract clearly studies Transformer language models and presents empirical evaluations showing that absolute position embeddings cause degraded performance when sentence positions are shifted—an analysis directly relevant to how TLMs encode/use word order/syntactic information (junior reviewers were split but the abstract provides sufficient empirical scope across models/tasks to meet inclusion).

Abstract describes empirical probing of syntactic information in language-model embeddings, introduces a new probe revealing redundant syntactic encodings, and shows performance gains from injecting syntax—evidence that the paper empirically analyzes syntax in LMs; however the abstract does not explicitly state the models are Transformer-based (junior reviewers split on this), so I lean to inclusion but conservatively (likely include).

論文對 GPT-2 small（Transformer LM）進行機械可解釋性分析，針對間接受詞識別（IOI）逆向工程了多個注意力頭並用因果干預驗證，這符合 TLM + 內部機制分析的範疇；兩位初審分別給 3 與 4（分歧在於摘要是否明確聲稱分析語法知識），因此在 recall 導向的篩選階段我傾向保留以進行全文審查，但摘要未明確指出“語法/句法分析”的具體實驗證據，需在全文中確認是否有直接評估句法結構或句法知識的實驗。

The abstract describes an empirical interpretability study that evaluates grammatical phenomena and model behavior in language generation—supporting syntactic analysis—but it does not explicitly state the models are Transformer-based, so I rate it 3 (advance for full review to verify the architecture); junior reviewers had split views (3 and 5).

The abstract reports empirical probing of GPT-3 on tense and the number of subjects/objects (tasks that implicate syntactic knowledge) so it likely fits the TLM + syntactic-assessment scope, but it frames the work as ‘semantic tasks’ and does not explicitly state analysis of syntactic structure or internal representations (junior reviewers gave 5 and 3), so there is some ambiguity—score 4 (可能納入).

The abstract proves theoretical semantic limitations of LLMs (Borel hierarchy) and cites empirical motivation but contains no mention of Transformer architectures, no empirical assessment of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge, and reads as a formal/theoretical paper—insufficient evidence to satisfy the inclusion criteria; therefore I rate it 3 (uncertain / needs more evidence).

The abstract describes a transformer-based modification with empirical evaluation (won BabyLM tracks), so it is relevant to TLMs and has empirical results, but it does not mention any assessment or analysis of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge (e.g., probing, syntactic benchmarks, or interpretability analyses of layers), so there is insufficient evidence to confirm it meets the syntactic-analysis criterion — conservatively keep for full review.
題名與摘要描述使用計算模型檢驗跨語言詞序與 UID（10 種語言）並報告結果，但並未提及 Transformer-based language models 或在 TLM 中對語法知識的實證評估；兩位初審均給 3，故依照 Stage 1 的高召回原則且缺少關鍵證據（是否使用 TLM、是否有針對語法的實驗），我評為 3。

Both junior reviewers gave a 3; the abstract describes empirical, data-driven linguistic curricula and evaluations aimed at probing models' linguistic knowledge (supporting potential relevance) but does not explicitly state that the experiments involve Transformer-based language models or that they specifically assess syntactic structure/metrics—missing these inclusion criteria, so retain for further review (uncertain).

The abstract describes empirical sparse probing of neuron activations in transformer LMs across scales (70M–6.9B) but does not explicitly state that the probed features are syntactic or that syntactic structure/knowledge is being analyzed, so inclusion is uncertain and more detail on which features/categories are syntactic is needed (both junior reviewers also scored 3).

Both junior reviewers gave 3; the abstract describes an empirical, morphologically-aware behavioral testing framework applied to ‘state-of-the-art language models’ across 12 typologically diverse languages (suggesting relevance to syntactic/morphological knowledge), but it does not explicitly state use of Transformer-based models nor clearly frame the evaluation as an analysis of syntactic structure—insufficient evidence to confidently include without the full text.

The abstract presents a new "Backpack" neural architecture and emphasizes interpretability, controllable generation, and comparisons to GPT-2, but it does not indicate the model is Transformer-based nor report any empirical assessment of syntactic structure/knowledge—so it likely fails the inclusion criteria (junior reviewers flagged similar missing evidence).
摘要描述對大型語言模型進行實證比較（metalinguistic prompting vs 直接機率量測）並有具體發現，但未明確指出使用 Transformer 架構亦未明確表示有針對語法結構/語法知識的實驗分析，證據不足以判定符合嚴格的語法評估納入標準，故給予中立評估。

The paper studies neuron-level interpretability in a Transformer LM (GPT-2 XL) and evaluates natural-language explanations both observationally and via interventions, but the abstract does not explicitly report any empirical analysis of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge (e.g., syntactic probes, dependency/generalization tests); both junior reviewers also marked this as uncertain, so more evidence is needed to confirm inclusion.

The title and abstract describe empirical interpretability work that uncovers grammatical rules (PCFG-trained models) and extends to a case study on language models—indicating analysis of linguistic/syntactic structure—but they do not explicitly state that the evaluated language models are Transformer-based, so inclusion with respect to TLMs is uncertain.

Both junior reviewers gave 3. The abstract describes transformer LMs and empirical analyses of internal processing (perplexity, attention, unit activations) when using machine vs. human prompts—so it is TLM-focused and interpretability-oriented—but it does not explicitly state that the study empirically assesses syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge (no mention of syntactic tasks, probes, or metrics), so evidence is insufficient to confirm inclusion at this stage.

I give score 3 (不確定). The title and abstract describe empirical diagnostic probing on Italian treebanks and the question of whether probes reveal syntactic competence in neural LMs (supporting relevance), but they do not explicitly state that Transformer-based language models are studied nor clearly confirm TLM-specific syntactic analyses—both junior reviewers likewise rated 3 for the same missing evidence; therefore more info (explicit mention of Transformer models and that the experiments analyze syntactic knowledge in TLMs) is needed to decide inclusion.

The abstract describes empirical crosslingual and monolingual structural-priming experiments in large multilingual language models that directly test abstract syntactic representations (supporting inclusion), but it does not explicitly state Transformer architectures so I conservatively mark it as possibly included; junior reviews were split (one cautious 3 noting missing architecture detail, one positive 5).
摘要說明對語言建模過程中九項任務（含語法）進行實證探究、跨訓練步與多個 seed 的 probing 分析，明確評估語法知識的出現與互動，符合對語法實證分析的要求；僅缺乏明示使用 Transformer 的字眼（兩位 junior reviewer 亦分別指出這一不確定性），但無負面證據，因此傾向保留以進入完整審查。

Title indicates a relevant topic (assessment of pre-trained models across languages and grammars), but the provided “abstract” is only citation metadata with no details about model types, empirical methods, or syntactic analyses; both junior reviewers also rated 3 and I agree—missing explicit evidence that (1) Transformer-based LMs are studied, (2) there is an empirical assessment of syntactic knowledge, and (3) concrete languages/grammars and evaluation methods are used.

The abstract reports an empirical, PCA-guided analysis of BERT (a Transformer) that explicitly examines morphosyntactic features and sentence-complexity-related signals in layer activations—meeting the inclusion criteria for syntactic/representational analysis in TLMs (junior reviewers differed, but the explicit mention of morphosyntactic features and activation-based empirical work supports strong inclusion).

Title/abstract indicate empirical work on alignment between human brains and language models using linguistic probing (supported by two junior reviews giving 3), but the abstract is incomplete and does not explicitly state Transformer-based models or clear empirical assessment of syntactic structure—missing explicit evidence of the required inclusion criteria, so remain uncertain and keep for full review.

3: The abstract (and the two junior reviewers) indicate an empirical study of LLMs and mention syntactic frames for the verb “break,” but it does not explicitly confirm Transformer-based models nor clearly describe empirical tests of syntactic knowledge or experimental methodology—so more detail is needed to meet the inclusion criteria.

Both junior reviewers gave 3; the abstract addresses non-linguistic skill injection in LLMs and claims linguistic knowledge retention but does not explicitly state use of Transformer architectures nor report any empirical assessment/analysis of syntactic structure (both required by the inclusion criteria), so evidence is insufficient—score 3 (needs full text to confirm).

Both junior reviewers noted relevant points (one cautious about semantics vs. syntax, one supportive), but the title+abstract clearly report experiments with Transformer-based BERT models using psycholinguistic data and explicit empirical evaluation of determiner prediction (a syntactic phenomenon) and anti-presuppositions, so this paper meets the inclusion criteria for full review.

The abstract presents JBLiMP and reports empirical syntactic evaluations that include GPT-2 (a Transformer) alongside LSTM and n-gram models, so it does assess syntactic knowledge in a TLM empirically, though it’s unclear whether the analysis centers on Transformer-specific behavior or internal representations—hence likely include for full review.

The BIG-bench paper clearly evaluates Transformer LMs across many tasks (so relevant to TLMs), but the abstract contains no explicit empirical assessment or analysis of syntactic structure/syntactic knowledge (both junior reviewers noted this), so there is insufficient evidence to include it now — score: 3.

The study uses a Transformer LM (BERT-large) and reports "linguistic probing," but the abstract does not explicitly state it assesses syntactic structure (e.g., constituency/dependency probes), so relevance to syntactic-knowledge criteria is uncertain—junior reviewers are split on this point; recommend advancing to full review only if the probes explicitly target syntax.

The abstract reports an empirical evaluation (new SR dataset), analysis, and proposed remedies targeting syntax understanding in text embedding models—indicating relevance and an empirical component—but it does not explicitly state that the evaluated models are Transformer-based, so inclusion is uncertain (junior reviews split 3 and 5); missing explicit evidence that the work studies Transformer-based language models.

The abstract reports an empirical comparative evaluation of Transformer-based LLMs (ChatGPT vs Google Gemini) on tasks explicitly testing syntax, grammar, vocabulary and reasoning—supporting inclusion—though it lacks detail on whether it probes syntactic structure or internal representations (junior reviewers split: one 5, one 3), so I rate 4 to advance to full review.

Both junior reviewers gave 3; the abstract describes large-scale language modeling across 250 languages and reports effects related to "syntactic similarity," which makes the paper potentially relevant, but it does not explicitly state use of Transformer architectures nor that it directly evaluates syntactic knowledge (e.g., probing, syntactic tasks or internal analyses) rather than correlating performance with linguistic similarity—insufficient evidence to confidently include.

The abstract reports an empirical, task-specific analysis of word-order and syntactic redundancy (meeting the syntactic-assessment criterion), but it never specifies that experiments target Transformer-based LMs (junior reviewers conflicted: one flagged this missing detail while the other assumed Transformers), so I cannot confirm inclusion without that key evidence — score 3 (needs more evidence).

The abstract describes an empirical interpretability method applied to a Transformer LM (Llama 2) that uncovers token-level information flows and explicitly reports similarities for tokens of the same part of speech and attention-head roles—evidence of syntactic analysis—so I recommend strong inclusion (junior reviews: one supportive (5) and one cautious (3), but the POS claim meets the inclusion criteria).

Junior reviewers both gave 3; the abstract reports empirical LM results including grammaticality judgments (which suggests a syntax-related evaluation) but does not explicitly state the use of Transformer-based models nor clearly describe direct analyses of syntactic structure, so evidence is insufficient—score 3 (needs explicit mention of Transformers and concrete syntactic analysis).
摘要報告大規模實驗檢驗語言模型 surprisal 對句法歧義處理的解釋力，這與關注語法知識的主題高度相關，但僅說明使用「兩種不同架構」的模型而未明確指出是否為 Transformer-based LM（兩位初評分別為 3 和 4），因此在缺乏明確證據下暫評為 3，建議閱全文以確認是否包含 Transformer 實驗後再決定納入。

Both junior reviewers gave 3; the abstract reports empirical LM simulations (perplexity differences tied to syntactic/parsing/memory biases) which suggests empirical work on syntactic phenomena, but it does not state the use of Transformer-based LMs nor whether it analyzes internal syntactic representations, so more information is needed to meet the inclusion criteria.

The abstract describes an empirical comparison of (large) language models and humans on recursively nested grammatical structures and reports experimental prompting effects—evidence of an empirical syntax assessment—but it does not explicitly state that the evaluated models are Transformer-based, so the key inclusion criterion (TLMs) is not fully confirmed; junior reviewers disagreed (one cautious 3 noting missing architecture info, one 5 assuming Transformers), so I conservatively mark it as uncertain pending explicit confirmation of model family.

The abstract reports an empirical study of syntactic knowledge in neural language models (human comparisons, training-data manipulations testing entrenchment and affectedness), so topic-relevant, but it does not specify the model architecture (Transformer vs RNN/LSTM), leaving the key inclusion criterion unverified—thus more info on the exact models used is needed.

The abstract studies LLM representations of truth (visualizations, transfer probes, causal interventions) and gives no indication of analyzing syntactic structure or explicitly focusing on Transformer architectures, so it does not meet the syntactic-analysis inclusion criterion based on title/abstract alone — retain as uncertain pending full text (both junior reviewers also scored 3).

Junior reviewers rated 3; the abstract describes interpretability methods for Transformer LMs (sparse feature circuits, SHIFT) but gives no explicit evidence of empirical assessment of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge, so the paper is uncertain for inclusion and needs full-text evidence—score 3.

The abstract empirically evaluates five Transformer LLMs on 11 tasks about how next-word prediction and probability shape model behavior, but it contains no explicit mention of assessing syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge (the required inclusion criterion), so there is insufficient evidence to include—score 3 (uncertain; needs explicit syntax-focused empirical analysis).

The title and abstract explicitly state the method is applied to Transformer LMs and reports empirical measures of linguistic generalization across a range of linguistic phenomena (comparing LSTM and Transformer results); although one junior reviewer was conservative about explicit syntactic claims, the abstract provides sufficient evidence of empirical evaluation of linguistic/syntactic generalization, so I recommend inclusion. (Junior reviews: one rated 3 noting ambiguity, the other rated 5.)

Both junior reviewers scored 3; the abstract describes an inference-time activation-steering method (ActAdd) to control topic/sentiment/detoxification in language models but does not mention any empirical assessment of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge (e.g., probing, syntactic benchmarks, or analyses of grammatical representations). Because the work appears focused on controllability rather than syntax, and the abstract lacks explicit evidence of empirical syntactic analysis, I cannot confidently include it—more evidence from the full paper about syntactic experiments would be needed.

Both junior reviewers rated 3; the abstract describes empirical interpretability analyses (linguistic task spaces, similarity probing, FTGD applied to LMs of three scales) but does not explicitly state the models are Transformer-based nor that it directly assesses syntactic structure—so uncertain and scored 3 pending confirmation of (a) Transformer architecture and (b) explicit syntax-focused experiments or benchmarks.

The abstract describes empirical, factorial tests on autoregressive deep-learning language models that probe filler–gap dependencies and island constraints—directly relevant to syntactic knowledge—so it should generally pass Stage 1; however the abstract does not explicitly state the model family (Transformer vs RNN/LSTM) or other architectural/training details (a concern noted by one junior reviewer), but absent negative evidence I favor keeping it for full review (aligned with the other junior reviewer who also saw it as relevant).

Both junior reviewers rated this as uncertain (3). The abstract studies ’linguistic competence’ and parameter regions in LLMs (likely Transformer-based) and reports cross-lingual and per-language effects, but it does not explicitly describe empirical analyses of syntactic structure (e.g., probing tasks, agreement tests, parse/treebank evaluations, or other syntax-specific benchmarks). Missing evidence: explicit mention of Transformer model family and concrete syntactic evaluation methods or results. Given the lack of explicit syntax-focused empirical details, I keep this as 3 (uncertain/needs more evidence).
我給 4（可能納入）：摘要描述了對 34 個訓練檢查點及多種模型尺寸的實證基準測試，並明確把「formal linguistic competence（語法/語言規則知識）」與腦對齊關聯起來，強烈暗示對語法/形式結構的實證分析（符合納入意圖）；但摘要未明言使用 Transformer 架構或具體的句法測試方法，故仍有資訊缺口需在全文確認。

Both junior reviewers gave 3; the abstract clearly studies Transformer LMs' pretraining dynamics, attention-head phase transitions, and alignment with human reading behavior using correlational and causal experiments, but it does not explicitly report any empirical assessment of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge, so evidence is insufficient to confirm inclusion — keep (3) to allow full-text verification.

The abstract reports empirical experiments on word-level and syntactic learning trajectories (lexical decision task) comparing subword and character language models, so it appears to assess syntax, but it does not specify that the models are Transformer-based (the key inclusion criterion); junior reviewers similarly flagged this uncertainty—therefore I give a cautious intermediate score.

The abstract describes experiments on “language models” and critical-period effects (including training manipulations and a plasticity regularizer) but does not specify Transformer-based models nor report any empirical assessment of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge, so there is insufficient evidence to determine inclusion under the criteria.
摘要探討 LLM 處理「非自然語言」並報告模型可泛化的潛在特徵與微調性能，但未明確呈現對語法結構或語法知識的實證分析或評估（兩位初審亦給出 3），故暫評為 3，需全文確認是否包含具體的語法實驗或分析證據。

Abstract examines Transformer LLMs' representations via activation-pattern (neurolinguistic) analyses and new minimal-pair datasets (COMPS-ZH/DE), which suggests empirical interpretability work; however it does not explicitly report empirical evaluation of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge (no mention of syntactic probes, syntactic generalization tests, or explicit syntax-focused metrics), so eligibility under the syntactic-knowledge criterion is unclear—both junior reviewers also rated 3. Recommend advancing to full review to confirm presence/absence of explicit syntactic analyses.

The abstract presents an empirical, syntax-targeted evaluation (CTSEG) and measures GEC model performance across grammar items—which aligns with assessing models' syntactic capabilities—but it does not explicitly state that the evaluated models are Transformer-based nor that it examines internal syntactic representations (the junior reviews also flagged this uncertainty), so I provisionally keep it for full review pending confirmation of Transformer architectures and explicit syntactic-analysis methods.

The abstract explicitly investigates LLM-based joint relation extraction and empirically evaluates the impact of incorporating constituency/dependency trees and semantic role labeling on extracted triplets—this addresses syntactic structures in LLMs (presumably Transformer-based) and reports empirical findings, with no evidence matching exclusion criteria; junior reviewers disagreed but the abstract supports inclusion.

Abstract describes extracting structural information from LLMs and reports empirical improvements on garden‑path sentence reading‑time prediction (evidence of empirical syntactic assessment), but it does not explicitly state use of Transformer‑based LMs (one junior reviewer flagged this uncertainty while another supported inclusion); therefore I lean to possible inclusion pending confirmation of model architecture.

The title and abstract describe human-scale language modeling and BabyLM small-data experiments relevant to computational psycholinguistics (as noted by the junior reviewers), but they do not explicitly state that Transformer-based models are used nor that the paper empirically assesses syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge—key inclusion criteria are therefore not confirmed, so retain for full review rather than exclude.

摘要報告了實證實驗：對比訓練/測試 LMs 在符合/違反語序類型學規律的自然主義反事實語言上的學習差異，直接關聯於語法學習偏好，因此主題相關且值得保留；但摘要未明確指出所用是否為 Transformer-based LMs，也未明確說明有無針對模型內部的語法結構實證分析，故無法確認是否滿足納入標準（需確認模型架構與是否有語法評估）。兩位初審也給出 3（不確定）。基於召回導向原則，建議進入全文審查以取更多證據。

Both junior reviewers scored 3; the abstract clearly concerns transformer LMs (RoBERTa) and reports empirical inspection of token vectors and tokenization effects—supporting topical relevance—but it does not explicitly describe any empirical evaluation of syntactic structure or syntactic knowledge (e.g., probing tasks, parsing/grammaticality assessments, or analyses of hierarchical dependencies), so inclusion is uncertain and warrants a cautious 3.
